title: Fermat pseudoprime
content:
In number theory, the Fermat pseudoprimes make up the most important class of pseudoprime s that come from Fermat's little theorem. Definition. Fermat's little theorem states that if "p" is prime and "a" is coprime to "p", then "a""p"−1 − 1 is divisible by "p". For an integer "a" 1, if a composite integer "x" divides "a""x"−1 − 1, then "x" is called a Fermat pseudoprime to base "a".

In other words, a composite integer is a Fermat pseudoprime to base "a" if it successfully passes the <a href="Fermat%20primality%20test" Fermat primality test</a for the base "a". The false statement that all numbers that pass the Fermat primality test for base 2, are prime, is called the <a href="Chinese%20hypothesis" Chinese hypothesis</a. The smallest base-2 Fermat pseudoprime is 341.

It is not a prime, since it equals 11·31, but it satisfies Fermat's little theorem: 2340 ≡ 1 (mod 341) and thus passes the <a href="Fermat%20primality%20test" Fermat primality test</a for the base 2. Pseudoprimes to base 2 are sometimes called Sarrus numbers, after <a href="Pierre%20Fr%C3%A9d%C3%A9ric%20Sarrus" P. F. Sarrus</a who discovered that 341 has this property, Poulet numbers, after <a href="Paul%20Poulet" P. Poulet</a who made a table of such numbers, or Fermatians.

A Fermat pseudoprime is often called a pseudoprime, with the modifier Fermat being understood. An integer "x" that is a Fermat pseudoprime for all values of "a" that are coprime to "x" is called a <a href="Carmichael%20number" Carmichael number</a. Properties. Distribution. There are infinitely many pseudoprimes to any given base "a" 1. In 1904, Cipolla showed how to produce an infinite number of pseudoprimes base "a" 1: Let "p" be a prime that does not divide "a"2 - 1.

Let "A" = ("a""p" - 1)/("a" - 1) and let "B" = ("a""p" + 1)/("a" + 1). Then "n" = "AB" is composite, and is a pseudoprime to base "a". For example, if "a" = 2 and "p" = 5, then "A" = 31, "B" = 11, and "n" = 341 is a pseudoprime to base 2.

In fact, there are infinitely many <a href="strong%20pseudoprime" strong pseudoprime</a s to any base greater than 1 (see Theorem 1 of) and infinitely many Carmichael numbers, but they are comparatively rare. There are three pseudoprimes to base 2 below 1000, 245 below one million, and 21853 less than 25·109. There are 4842 strong pseudoprimes base 2 and 2163 Carmichael numbers below this limit (see Table 1 of).

Starting at 17·257, the product of consecutive Fermat numbers is a base-2 pseudoprime, and so are all <a href="Fermat%20prime" Fermat composite</a and <a href="Mersenne%20prime" Mersenne composite</a. Factorizations. The factorizations of the 60 Poulet numbers up to 60787, including 13 Carmichael numbers (in bold), are in the following table. A Poulet number all of whose divisors "d" divide 2"d" − 2 is called a <a href="super-Poulet%20number" super-Poulet number</a. There are infinitely many Poulet numbers which are not super-Poulet Numbers. Smallest Fermat pseudoprimes.

The smallest pseudoprime for each base "a" ≤ 200 is given in the following table; the colors mark the number of prime factors. Unlike in the definition at the start of the article, pseudoprimes below "a" are excluded in the table. (For that to allow pseudoprimes below "a", see) List of Fermat pseudoprimes in fixed base "n".

For more information (base 31 to 100), see to, and for all bases up to 150, see <a href="http%3A//de.m.wikibooks.org/wiki/Pseudoprimzahlen%3A_Tabelle_Fermatsche_Pseudoprimzahlen" table of Fermat pseudoprimes (text in German)</a, this page does not define "n" is a pseudoprime to a base congruent to 1 or -1 (mod "n") Which bases "b" make "n" a Fermat pseudoprime? If composite formula_1 is even, then formula_1 is a Fermat pseudoprime to the trivial base formula_3. If composite formula_1 is odd, then formula_1 is a Fermat pseudoprime to the trivial bases formula_6.

For any composite formula_1, the "number" of distinct bases formula_8 modulo formula_1, for which formula_1 is a Fermat pseudoprime base formula_8, is where formula_13 are the distinct prime factors of formula_1. This includes the trivial bases. For example, for formula_15, this product is formula_16. For formula_17, the smallest such nontrivial base is formula_18. Every odd composite formula_1 is a Fermat pseudoprime to at least two nontrivial bases modulo formula_1 unless formula_1 is a power of 3. For composite "n" < 200, the following is a table of all bases "b" < "n" which "n" is a Fermat pseudoprime.

If a composite number "n" is not in the table (or "n" is in the sequence), then "n" is a pseudoprime only to the trivial base 1 modulo "n". For more information ("n" = 201 to 5000), see, this page does not define "n" is a pseudoprime to a base congruent to 1 or -1 (mod "n").

When "p" is a prime, "p"2 is a Fermat pseudoprime to base "b" <a href="if%20and%20only%20if" if and only if</a "p" is a <a href="Wieferich%20prime" Wieferich prime</a to base "b". For example, 10932 = 1194649 is a Fermat pseudoprime to base 2, and 112 = 121 is a Fermat pseudoprime to base 3.
